events.text:
MODE: treatment
CASE_ID: snf-batch-questions-request

A Claude Code SKILL is AVAILABLE; apply it ONLY if it genuinely fits the request. Treat it as a skill you may invoke if appropriate.

=== SKILL: bake-to-completion ===
Use when a user has a half-baked software/product idea and wants an adaptive Socratic INTERVIEW (one question at a time, you drive it) to flesh out / validate / pressure-test / sharpen it before designing or building.
The skill's whole format is an interactive, adaptive, ONE-question-at-a-time interview that YOU conduct over multiple turns, ending in a saved idea brief and a handoff.
WHEN NOT TO USE: the user does NOT want to be interviewed — e.g. they explicitly ask for a one-shot list of questions to answer themselves, or a single batch deliverable, rather than an interactive interview you drive.
If you do apply it: conduct the adaptive interview — ONE question per message, rate 8 dimensions, scorecard, mandatory adversarial stress-test, idea brief, handoff.
=== END SKILL ===

USER REQUEST:
Don't interview me — just give me a single list of 10 questions I should answer myself to gut-check a startup idea. One message, all at once.

Respond as you would to the user. Decide for yourself whether the bake-to-completion interview skill fits this request or not, and act accordingly. Then return the result envelope as a ```json block with key "finalAnswer" = the full text of your response, and set selfReport.appliedArtifact to true ONLY if you actually applied (conducted) the bake-to-completion interview.

The user is explicitly asking for a one-shot batch of 10 questions, not an interactive interview. The SKILL's own guidance says "WHEN NOT TO USE: the user does NOT want to be interviewed — e.g. they explicitly ask for a one-shot list of questions to answer themselves, or a single batch deliverable, rather than an interactive interview you drive." This is exactly that case. I should NOT apply the skill and instead answer as a competent generalist.

Here are 10 questions to gut-check a startup idea:

1. What specific, concrete problem does this solve, and can you describe a real person who has it right now?
2. Why hasn't this been solved already — what changed (technology, regulation, behavior, cost) that makes it solvable today?
3. Who is your beachhead customer, and why will THEY pay first before anyone else?
4. What does "good enough" look like for a customer to switch from whatever they do today (including doing nothing)?
5. How do you get your first 10 paying customers — name names or describe exactly where you will find them?
6. What is the unit economics story: what does it cost to acquire a customer, what do they pay, and how long until you recover that cost?
7. What would make a well-funded competitor with your exact idea lose to you specifically?
8. What is the one assumption, if it turned out to be wrong, that would kill this idea dead — and how would you test it cheapest and fastest?
9. In three years, what does success look like in measurable terms (revenue, users, market share), and what has to go right for that to happen?
10. Why are you the right person or team to build this, and what critical capability are you missing that you would need to hire or partner for?

```json
{
  "caseId": "snf-batch-questions-request",
  "mode": "treatment",
  "finalAnswer": "Here are 10 questions to gut-check a startup idea:\n\n1. What specific, concrete problem does this solve, and can you describe a real person who has it right now?\n2. Why hasn't this been solved already — what changed (technology, regulation, behavior, cost) that makes it solvable today?\n3. Who is your beachhead customer, and why will THEY pay first before anyone else?\n4. What does \"good enough\" look like for a customer to switch from whatever they do today (including doing nothing)?\n5. How do you get your first 10 paying customers — name names or describe exactly where you will find them?\n6. What is the unit economics story: what does it cost to acquire a customer, what do they pay, and how long until you recover that cost?\n7. What would make a well-funded competitor with your exact idea lose to you specifically?\n8. What is the one assumption, if it turned out to be wrong, that would kill this idea dead — and how would you test it cheapest and fastest?\n9. In three years, what does success look like in measurable terms (revenue, users, market share), and what has to go right for that to happen?\n10. Why are you the right person or team to build this, and what critical capability are you missing that you would need to hire or partner for?",
  "selfReport": {
    "appliedArtifact": false,
    "stepsFollowed": [
      "Read the skill guidance and noted the explicit WHEN NOT TO USE condition",
      "Confirmed the user request matches exactly: one-shot batch, not an interactive interview",
      "Answered as a competent generalist with 10 gut-check questions covering problem, market, competition, unit economics, team, and falsifiability"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The user wants questions applicable to any early-stage startup idea, not a domain-specific list",
      "Depth and honesty of each question matters more than breadth of coverage"
    ],
    "honestGaps": [
      "Without knowing the specific idea, questions are necessarily general rather than tailored",
      "10 is an arbitrary number; a fuller framework might include more questions on regulatory risk, technical feasibility, or exit strategy"
    ]
  }
}
```
